name: Empress Jingu
description: Empress Jingu, a semi-legendary figure in Japanese history, is revered as a powerful and enigmatic ruler who is believed to have reigned from 201 to 269 AD. Her existence is debated among historians, with some regarding her as a purely mythical figure, while others consider her a historical personage whose exploits were embellished over time. She is primarily known for her alleged conquest of Silla, one of the ancient Korean kingdoms, a campaign that is heavily intertwined with myth and legend. Jingu is often depicted as a warrior queen, embodying strength, strategic brilliance, and divine favor. Her story is a complex blend of political ambition, supernatural intervention, and historical ambiguity, solidifying her place in Japanese folklore and national identity. She serves as a symbol of female leadership and martial prowess, though the veracity of her achievements remains a subject of scholarly discussion. Her legacy is complex, intertwined with themes of expansionism and national identity.
subtitle: Warrior Empress

keyMyths.title: The Conquest of Silla
keyMyths.description: Empress Jingu's most famous legend revolves around her conquest of Silla. According to the Kojiki and Nihon Shoki, she was divinely inspired to invade Korea. Preparing for war while pregnant, she miraculously suppressed her pregnancy for three years until the campaign's successful completion. Leading a powerful fleet, she subdued Silla without bloodshed, securing tribute and establishing Japanese dominance over the Korean peninsula. This narrative has been interpreted both as a literal historical account and as a symbolic representation of Japanese power and influence.

keyMyths.title: Divine Guidance and Omens
keyMyths.description: Numerous myths surround Empress Jingu's reign and her connection to the divine. Before embarking on her conquest, she received omens and guidance from various deities, including the sun goddess Amaterasu. These divine interventions are often depicted as crucial to her success, highlighting the belief that she was acting with the support of the gods. The myths reinforce her legitimacy as a ruler and emphasize the importance of divine favor in achieving political and military objectives.

keyMyths.title: The Suppressed Pregnancy
keyMyths.description: A central element of Empress Jingu's legend is her ability to suppress her pregnancy for three years during the Silla campaign. This miraculous feat is often interpreted as a symbol of her exceptional strength, determination, and dedication to her duties as a ruler. It reinforces her image as a figure who transcends the limitations of ordinary women, capable of extraordinary feats through sheer willpower and divine assistance. This also sets the stage for the birth of her son, Emperor Ojin, who is also considered a deity.
keyMyths.source: Aston, W.G. (translator). Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697.

sources.author: Aston, W.G.
sources.work: Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697
sources.description: An English translation of the Nihon Shoki, one of the earliest chronicles of Japanese history. Provides detailed accounts of Empress Jingu's reign and exploits.
sources.text: An English translation of the Nihon Shoki, one of the earliest chronicles of Japanese history. Provides detailed accounts of Empress Jingu's reign and exploits.

sources.author: Philippi, Donald L.
sources.description: An English translation of the Kojiki, another foundational text of Japanese mythology and history. Offers a different perspective on Jingu's life and the events surrounding her.

sources.author: Kitagawa, Joseph M.
sources.description: Provides scholarly analysis of the religious and mythological context surrounding Empress Jingu and her significance in Japanese culture.
